California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 18, 1989


Claudia Foutz
Chief Deputy Director
Department of Consumer Affairs
Legal Office
1020 N Street, Room 510
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-89-233

Dear Ms. Foutz:

This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
the Department of Consumer Affairs regarding reporting obligations
of board members, board staff, and proctors under the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your request is general in
nature and does not apply to one specific set of facts, we are
providing you with informal assistance applicable to future
conduct and make no reference to past conduct.²

QUESTIONS

1. Are board members, board staff, or proctors required to
report on the annual Statement of Economic Interests, Form 730,
that they have received a discounted room from a hotel?

2. How is the amount which must be reported determined?

CONCLUSION

1. Designated employees are required to report discounted
hotel accommodations as gifts unless similar discounts are
available to the general public without regard to official status.

_______________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity granted by formal advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(4)(c),
copy enclosed.)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. I-89-233
Page 2

2. The value of the gift is the difference between the fair
market value of the accommodations and the sums paid by agency
officials.

FACTS

Boards within the Department of Consumer Affairs administer
licensing examinations. As the need arises, these boards contract
with hotels by renting a large room or rooms where the examination
will be administered. A board staffer negotiates these written
contracts. Appropriate contract forms are completed for the
rental of the examination rooms and submitted to the business
services office of the Department of Consumer Affairs for processing and approval.

Concurrently, boards negotiate a rate for sleeping accommodations at the same hotel for board members, staff and proctors
involved in the administration of the examination. The hotel
either offers a discount account for the sleeping accommodations or
provides complimentary accommodations for board members, staff and
proctors because of the number of rooms being committed for the
examination. This arrangement is oral. The sleeping accommodations
are not addressed in the written contract forms completed for the
rental of examination rooms. Your agency reimburses board
members, staff and proctors at a fixed rate on a per diem basis.

Examination candidates also typically arrange for sleeping
accommodations at the same hotel. Examination candidates are not
provided discounted rooms.

Board members and executive officers of licensing boards in
the Department of Consumer Affairs are designated employees pursuant to subdivisions (a) and (c) of Section 82019. At larger
boards, certain staffers under the executive officer may also be
designated employees for purposes of the Conflict of Interest Code
of the Department of Consumer Affairs pursuant to subdivisions (c)
and (d) of Section 82019. Proctors are not designated employees.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act requires a public official to make
certain financial disclosures as required by his or her agency's
conflict of interest code. (Section 87302.) With extremely
limited exceptions, under the Department of Consumer Affairs
Conflict of Interest Code, designated employees of each board must
report income if the source of the income is a person (individual,
partnership, firm, corporation, or association) "(E)ngaged in
tendering services or selling products to the particular division,
board, bureau, committee, or commission, or a manufacturer,
wholesaler, or distributor of such products." (Department of
Consumer Affairs, Conflict of Interest Code, Disclosure Category
No. 9.)

File No. I-89-233
Page 3

Section 82028 defines the term "gift" to include a discount
or rebate if given to a public official unless given without
regard to the "official status" of the public official and on
terms available to members of the public. You have indicated in
your letter that examinees pay full price for their accommoda-
tions. You have not indicated whether other members of the public
who contract for meeting rooms are offered discounts or
complimentary sleeping accommodations at these hotels. If
discounts and complimentary sleeping accommodations are offered
consistently to other members of the public who contract for meet-
ing rooms, then the discounts are made available without regard to
official status and are not considered gifts for purposes of the
Act. Conversely, if the similar discounts are available to members
of the public, then the discounts are gifts to the public of-
ficials. (In re Russell (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 191, copy enclosed.)

If the discounts offered to designated employees of your
agency constitute gifts, they should be reported in the employees'
statements of economic interests. The value of each gift is the
difference between the fair market value of the accommodations as
determined by rates quoted by the establishment to other clients
and the amounts paid by the public employees. (Regulation
18726(a), In re Hopkins, 3 FPPC Ops. 107, 112, copies enclosed.)

Additionally, the Political Reform Act prohibits a public
official from making, participating in or using his or her of-
ficial position to influence a governmental decision in which he
or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) A public of-
ficial has a financial interest in a decision if the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on an
official's economic interest including:

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

Section 87103(e).

As used in the Act, the term public official includes every
officer or employee of a governmental entity. (Section 82048.)
Consequently, this prohibition applies to all board members and
all employees of the Department of Consumer Affairs. Thus, if the
discounted rooms are considered gifts to the officials who use
them, and the value received from a single source equals $250 or
more during the 12 months preceding a governmental decision, the
recipient of the discounted rooms must disqualify himself or
herself from participating in the decision if it materially
affects the donor. This includes decisions to contract for
sleeping accommodations or meeting rooms from the donor.

File No. I-89-233
Page 4


     I trust this letter is responsive to your inquiry.  Should
you have any further questions regarding the above, do not
hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Kathryn E. Donovan
                                        General Counsel


                                        By: Blanca M. Breeze
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

KED:BMB:plh

Enclosures

State of California

Memorandum

To      'FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION              Date : April 14, 1989
        Attn: Legal Division
        428 J Street, Suite 800                          Subject: Request for
        P.O. Box 807                                              Letter Advice
        Sacramento, CA  95804


From :  Department of Consumer Affairs
        Legal Office


        This is to request advice of the Fair Political Practices
Commission in the form of a letter opinion addressing the following
situation. The specific situation is factual and not hypothetical.
We have chosen to set forth the facts in a somewhat generic sense
because there may be similar factual situations with more than one
licensing board in the Department of Consumer Affairs (DCA).

Facts

A board within DCA must administer a licensing examination.  It
contracts with a large hotel by renting a large room or rooms where
the exam will be administered.  A board staffer negotiates the
contract for the examination room(s).  At the same time, a rate
is negotiated for sleeping rooms at the same hotel for board
members, staff and proctors involved in the exam's administration.
The hotel either discounts the sleeping room rate or provides
complimentary rooms for members, staff and proctors because of the
number of rooms being committed for the examination.  In the
particular situation giving rise to this request, examination
candidates also typically rent sleeping rooms at the same hotel.
Candidates are not provided discount rooms.

Issues

Our questions are as follows:

        1.  Are board members, board staff, or proctors required to
report on the annual Statement of Economic Interests Form 730 that
they have received a discounted room from the hotel?

        2.  If the answer with regard to any of the parties in
question No. 1 is "yes", how is the amount which must be reported
determined?

Additional Background Information

We are providing additional background information to aid in your
analysis and response.

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
April 14, 1989
Page 2


Board members and Executive Officers of licensing boards in DCA are
designated employees pursuant to subdivisions (a) and (c) of
Government Code Section 82019. At larger boards, certain staffers
under the Executive Officer may also be designated employees in
DCA's Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to subdivisions (c) and
(d) of Government Code Section 82019. Proctors are not designated
employees.

With extremely limited exception, under the Department of Consumer
Affairs Conflict of Interest Code, designated employees of each
board must report income if the source of the income is a person
(individual, partnership, firm, corporation, or association):

"Engaged in rendering services or selling products to the
particular * * board * * *." (Disclosure Category No. 11.)

For your ready reference, we have enclosed a copy of the DCA
Conflict of Interest Code.

Finally, we note that the Department of Consumer Affairs
contracting process is somewhat unique. The process for the rental
of an examination site is as follows: Typically an Executive
Officer will contact various hotels to determine if their
facilities can accommodate the board's needs. Once a hotel is
chosen, rates are negotiated. Appropriate contract forms are then
completed for the rental of the examination room(s), and submitted
to DCA's Business Services Office for processing and approval. The
rooms for board members, board staff and proctors are not addressed
in the contract. These are merely oral agreements. Under Business
and Professions Code Section 156, the Director of the Department
of Consumer Affairs must sign off on contracts. He has delegated
actual sign off authority to the DCA Fiscal Officer. So it is
actually the department which is entering into the exam site rental
contract on behalf of the board. The exam is held, the bill is
submitted and is paid, the funds coming from the board's special
fund.

We trust the foregoing information is adequate to respond to our
inquiry. If you have any questions or wish to further discuss any
of the foregoing, please contact me at your convenience.

Claudia Foutz
CLAUDIA FOUTZ
Chief Deputy Director

TITLE 16                    CONSUMER AFFAIRS                § 3830
[Register 85, No. 4—1-26-85]                                          (p. 601)

CHAPTER 38. DEPARTMENT OF CONSUMER AFFAIRS
(Originally printed 4-2-77)

SUBCHAPTER 1. (RESERVED)
SUBCHAPTER 2. DEPARTMENT OF CONSUMER AFFAIRS—
               CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE
SUBCHAPTER 3. IMMIGRATION CONSULTANTS

SUBCHAPTER 2. DEPARTMENT OF CONSUMER AFFAIRS—
CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE

DETAILED ANALYSIS

Section
3830.        General Provisions
             Appendix

3830. General Provisions.

The Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000, et seq., require
state and local government agencies to adopt and promulgate Conflict of Inter-
est Codes. The Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted a regulation, 2
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730, which contains the terms of a standard Conflict
of Interest Code, which can be incorporated by reference, and which may be
amended by the Fair Political Practices Commission to conform to amend-
ments in the Political Reform Act after public notice and hearings. Therefore,
the terms of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730 and any amendments to it duly
adopted by the Fair Political Practices Commission, along with the attached
Appendix in which officials and employees are designated and disclosure categ-
ories are set forth, are hereby incorporated by reference and constitute the
Conflict of Interest Code of the Department of Consumer Affairs.

Pursuant to Section 4(A) of the standard Code, designated employees shall
file statements of economic interests with the agency. Upon receipt of the
statement of the Director, the agency shall make and keep a copy and forward
the original of this statement to the Fair Political Practices Commission.

NOTE: Authority cited: Sections 87300 and 87304, Government Code. Reference: Section
87300 et seq., Government Code.

HISTORY:
1. New Chapter 38 (Sections 3830-3880 and Appendix A) filed 4-1-77; effective thirtieth
   day thereafter. Approved by Fair Political Practices Commission 3-17-77 (Register 77,
   No. 14).
2. Repealer of Chapter 38 (Sections 3830-3880 and Appendix A) and new Chapter 38
   (Section 3830 and Appendix) filed 2-29-81; effective thirtieth day thereafter. Approved
   by Fair Political Practices Commission 12-1-80 (Register 81, No. 9).

    Consultants shall disclose financial interests pursuant to the disclosure catego-
ries applicable to the unit that contracts for their services.

Disclosure Categories
    As used in the following disclosure categories, "person" includes, but is not
limited to, individuals, partnerships, firms, corporations, and association. A
designated employee required to report under one or more of the following
disclosure categories must disclose investments, interests in real property and
income if the business entity in which the investment is held, the real property,
or the source of the income is:

5—82096

§ 3830
(p. 606)

CONSUMER AFFAIRS

TITLE 16
(Register 81, No. 9—2-28-81)

1. Regulated by one or more of the agencies within the department.
2. Engaged in rendering services or selling products to the department, or
a manufacturer, wholesaler or distributor of such products.
3. A person against whom the department is contemplating legal or ad-
ministrative action, has legal or administrative action pending, or has
intervened in such action.
4. A manufacturer, wholesaler or retailer of products or equipment ap-
proved or designated for use by any agency within the department.
5. A manufacturer, wholesaler or retailer of products or equipment re-
quired to meet standards set by any agency within the department.
6. A person, school, college or university offering individual courses and/or
a curriculum which must be approved by any agency within the depart-
ment.
7. A person, school, college or university which prepares, administers, or
grades examinations given by any agency within the department.
8. Engaged in the business or profession regulated by the particular board,
bureau, committee, commission, or division.
9. Engaged in rendering services or selling products to the particular divi-
sion, board, bureau, committee, or commission, or a manufacturer,
wholesaler, or distributor of such products.
10. A manufacturer, wholesaler, or retailer of products or equipment ap-
proved or designated for use by the particular board, bureau, committee,
or commission.
11. A manufacturer, wholesaler, or retailer of products or equipment re-
quired to meet standards set by the particular board, bureau, committee,
or commission.
12. A person, school, college or university offering individual courses and/or
a curriculum which is approved by the particular board, committee, or
bureau.
13. A person, school, college, or university which prepares, administers, or
grades examinations given by the particular board, committee, or bureau.
14. Engaged in a business or profession affected by pending legislation when
recommendations may be made by the particular division, board, bureau,
committee, or commission regarding such legislation.
15. Engaged in a business or profession affected by pending or proposed
legislation when recommendations may be made by the department
regarding such legislation.
16. Any investments, interests in real property and sources of income.
17. A facility licensed by the Department of Health which must be managed
by a nursing home administrator.

§ 156.    Contracts
    The director may, at the request and with the consent of the par-
ticular board within the department on whose behalf the contract is
to be made, enter into contracts pursuant to Article 4 of Chapter 3 of
Part 1 of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code ¹ for and on
behalf of any board within the department.

(Added by Stats.1953, c. 864, p. 2211, § 1.)

Memorandum

To : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
Attn: Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Date : April 14, 1989

Subject: Request for
Letter Advice

From : Department of Consumer Affairs
Legal Office

This is to request advice of the Fair Political Practices
Commission in the form of a letter opinion addressing the following
situation. The specific situation is factual and not hypothetical.
We have chosen to set forth the facts in a somewhat generic sense
because there may be similar factual situations with more than one
licensing board in the Department of Consumer Affairs (DCA).

Facts

A board within DCA must administer a licensing examination. It
contracts with a large hotel by renting a large room or rooms where
the exam will be administered. A board staffer negotiates the
contract for the examination room(s). At the same time, a rate
is negotiated for sleeping rooms at the same hotel for board
members, staff and proctors involved in the exam's administration.
The hotel either discounts the sleeping room rate or provides
complimentary rooms for members, staff and proctors because of the
number of rooms being committed for the examination. In the
particular situation giving rise to this request, examination
candidates also typically rent sleeping rooms at the same hotel.
Candidates are not provided discount rooms.

Issues

Our questions are as follows:

1. Are board members, board staff, or proctors required to
report on the annual Statement of Economic Interests Form 730 that
they have received a discounted room from the hotel?

2. If the answer with regard to any of the parties in
question No. 1 is "yes", how is the amount which must be reported
determined?

Additional Background Information

We are providing additional background information to aid in your
analysis and response.

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
April 14, 1989
Page 2


Board members and Executive Officers of licensing boards in DCA are
designated employees pursuant to subdivisions (a) and (c) of
Government Code Section 82019. At larger boards, certain staffers
under the Executive Officer may also be designated employees in
DCA's Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to subdivisions (c) and
(d) of Government Code Section 82019. Proctors are not designated
employees.

With extremely limited exception, under the Department of Consumer
Affairs Conflict of Interest Code, designated employees of each
board must report income if the source of the income is a person
(individual, partnership, firm, corporation, or association):

"Engaged in rendering services or selling products to the
particular * * * board * * *." (Disclosure Category No. 11.)

For your ready reference, we have enclosed a copy of the DCA
Conflict of Interest Code.

Finally, we note that the Department of Consumer Affairs
contracting process is somewhat unique. The process for the rental
of an examination site is as follows: Typically an Executive
Officer will contact various hotels to determine if their
facilities can accommodate the board's needs. Once a hotel is
chosen, rates are negotiated. Appropriate contract forms are then
completed for the rental of the examination room(s), and submitted
to DCA's Business Services Office for processing and approval. The
rooms for board members, board staff and proctors are not addressed
in the contract. These are merely oral agreements. Under Business
and Professions Code Section 156, the director of the Department
of Consumer Affairs must sign off on contracts. He has delegated
actual sign off authority to the DCA Fiscal Officer. So is
actually the department which is entering into the exam site rental
contract on behalf of the board. The exam is held, the bill is
submitted and is paid, the funds coming from the board's special
fund.

We trust the foregoing information is adequate to respond to our
inquiry. If you have any questions or wish to further discuss any
of the foregoing, please contact me at your convenience.

[signature]

CLAUDIA FOUTZ
Chief Deputy Director

TITLE 16 CONSUMER AFFAIRS § 3830
[Register 85, No. 4-1-25-88] (p. 601)

CHAPTER 38. DEPARTMENT OF CONSUMER AFFAIRS
(Originally printed 4-2-77)

SUBCHAPTER 1. (RESERVED)
SUBCHAPTER 2. DEPARTMENT OF CONSUMER AFFAIRS—
CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE
SUBCHAPTER 3. IMMIGRATION CONSULTANTS

SUBCHAPTER 2. DEPARTMENT OF CONSUMER AFFAIRS—
CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE

DETAILED ANALYSIS

Section
3830. General Provisions
Appendix

3830. General Provisions.
The Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000, et seq., require
state and local government agencies to adopt and promulgate Conflict of Interest Codes. The Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted a regulation, 2
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730, which contains the terms of a standard Conflict
of Interest Code, which can be incorporated by reference, and which may be
amended by the Fair Political Practices Commission to conform to amendments in the Political Reform Act after public notice and hearings. Therefore,
the terms of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730 and any amendments to it duly
adopted by the Fair Political Practices Commission, along with the attached
Appendix in which officials and employees are designated and disclosure categories are set forth, are hereby incorporated by reference and constitute the
Conflict of Interest Code of the Department of Consumer Affairs.

Pursuant to Section 4(A) of the standard Code, designated employees shall
file statements of economic interests with the agency. Upon receipt of the
statement of the Director, the agency shall make and retain a copy and forward
the original of this statement to the Fair Political Practices Commission.

NOTE: Authority cited: Sections 87300 and 87304, Government Code. Reference: Section
87300 et seq., Government Code.

HISTORY:
1. New Chapter 38 (Sections 3830-3890 and Appendix A) filed 4-1-77; effective thirtieth
day thereafter. Approved by Fair Political Practices Commission 3-17-77 (Register 77,
No. 14).
2. Repealer of Chapter 38 (Sections 3830-3890 and Appendix A) and new Chapter 38
(Section 3830 and Appendix) filed 2-26-81; effective thirtieth day thereafter. Approved
by Fair Political Practices Commission 12-1-80 (Register 81, No. 9).

   Consultants shall disclose financial interests pursuant to the disclosure catego-
ries applicable to the unit that contracts for their services.

Disclosure Categories

   As used in the following disclosure categories, "person" includes, but is not
limited to, individuals, partnerships, firms, corporations, and associations. A
designated employee required to report under one or more of the following
disclosure categories must disclose investments, interests in real property and
income if the business entity in which the investment is held, the real property,
or the source of the income is:

S—82096

1. 
Are board members, board staff, or proctors required to 
report on the annual Statement of Economic Interests, Form 730, 
that they have received a discounted room from a hotel? 
2. 
How is the amount which must be reported determined?
sections.conclusion:
1. 
Designated employees are required to report discounted 
hotel accommodations as gifts unless similar discounts are 
available to the general public without regard to official status. 
, et seq.

Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

sections.facts:
Boards within the Department of Consumer Affairs administer 
licensing examinations. 
As the need arises, these boards contract 
with hotels by renting a large room or rooms where the examination 
will be administered. 
A board staffer negotiates these written 
contracts. 
Appropriate contract forms are completed for the 
rental of the examination rooms and submitted to the business 
services office of the Department of Consumer Affairs for process-
ing and approval. 
Concurrently, boards negotiate a rate for sleeping accommoda-
tions at the same hotel for board members, staff and proctors 
involved in the administration of the examination. 
The hotel 
either offers a discount for the sleeping accommodations or 
provides complimentary accommodations for board members, staff and 
proctors because of the number of rooms being committed for the 
examination. 
This agreement is oral. 
The sleeping accommodations 
are not addressed in the written contract forms completed for the 
rental of examination rooms. 
Your agency reimburses board 
members, staff and proctors at a fixed rate on a per diem basis. 
Examination candidates also typically arrange for sleeping 
accommodations at the same hotel. 
Examination candidates are not 
provided discounted rooms. 
Board members and executive officers of licensing boards in 
the Department of Consumer Affairs are designated employees pursu-
ant to subdivisions (a) and (c) of Section 82019. 
At larger 
boards, certain staffers under the executive officer may also be 
designated employees for purposes of the Conflict of Interest Code 
of the Department of Consumer Affairs pursuant to subdivisions (c) 
and (d) of Section 82019. 
Proctors are not designated employees.
sections.analysis:
The Political Reform Act requires a public official to make 
certain financial disclosures as required by his or her agency's 
conflict of interest code. 
(Section 87302.) 
With extremely 
limited exceptions, under the Department of Consumer Affairs 
Conflict of Interest Code, designated employees of each board must 
report income if the source of the income is a person (individual, 
partnership, firm, corporation, or association) "(E)ngaged in 
rendering services or selling products to the particular division, 
board, bureau, committee, or commission, or a manufacturer, 
wholesaler, or distributor of such products." 
(Department of 
Consumer Affairs, Conflict of Interest Code, Disclosure Category 
No.9. )  
section 82028 defines the term "gift" to include a discount 
or rebate if given to a public official unless given without 
regard to the "official status" of the public official and on 
terms available to members of the public. 
You have indicated in 
your letter that examinees pay full price for their accommoda-
tions. 
You have not indicated whether other members of the public 
who contract for meeting rooms are offered discounts or 
complimentary sleeping accommodations at these hotels. 
If 
discounts and complimentary sleeping accommodations are offered 
consistently to other members of the public who contract for meet-
ing rooms, then the discounts are made available without regard to 
official status and are not considered gifts for purposes of the 
Act. 
Conversely, if no similar discounts are available to members 
of the public, then the discounts are gifts to the public of-
ficials. 
(In re Russell (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 191, copy enclosed.) 
If the discounts offered to designated employees of your 
agency constitute gifts, they should be reported in the employees' 
statements of economic interests. 
The value of each gift is the 
difference between the fair market value of the accommodations as 
determined by rates quoted by the establishment to other clients 
and the amounts paid by the public employees. 
(Regulation 
18726(a), In re Hopkins, 3 FPPC Ops. 107, 112, copies enclosed.) 
Additionally, the Political Reform Act prohibits a public 
official from making, participating in or using his or her of-
ficial position to influence a governmental decision in which he 
or she has a financial interest. 
(Section 87100.) 
A public of-
ficial has a financial interest in a decision if the decision will 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, 
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on an 
official's economic interest inc~uding: 
(e) 
Any donor of, or any intermediary or 
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating 
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value 
provided to, received by, or promised to the public 
official within 12 months prior to the time when 
the decision is made. 
Section 87103(e). 
As used in the Act, the term public official includes every 
officer or employee of a governmental entity. 
(Section 82048.) 
Consequently, this prohibition applies to all board members and 
all employees of the Department of Consumer Affairs. 
Thus, if the 
discounted rooms are considered gifts to the officials who use 
them, and the value received from a single source equals $250 or 
more during the 12 months preceding a governmental decision, the 
recipient of the discounted rooms must disqualify himself or 
herself from participating in the decision if it materially 
affects the donor. 
This includes decisions to contract for 
sleeping accommodations or meeting rooms from the donor.  
I trust this letter is responsive to your inquiry. 
Should 
you have any further questions regarding the above, do not 
hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
KED:BMB:plh 
Enclosures

report on the annual Statement of Economic Interests, Form 730, 
that they have received a discounted room from a hotel? 
2.

Designated employees are required to report discounted 
hotel accommodations as gifts unless similar discounts are 
available to the general public without regard to official status.

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission July 18, 1989 Claudia Foutz Chief Deputy Director Department of Consumer Affairs Legal Office 1020 N Street, Room 510 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-89-233 Dear Ms. Foutz: This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Department of Consumer Affairs regarding reporting obligations of board members, board staff, and proctors under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your request is general in nature and does not apply to one specific set of facts, we are providing you with informal assistance applicable to future conduct and make no reference to past conduct.² QUESTIONS 1. Are board members, board staff, or proctors required to report on the annual Statement of Economic Interests, Form 730, that they have received a discounted room from a hotel? 2. How is the amount which must be reported determined? CONCLUSION 1. Designated employees are required to report discounted hotel accommodations as gifts unless similar discounts are available to the general public without regard to official status. _______________________ ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity granted by formal advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(4)(c), copy enclosed.) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 File No. I-89-233 Page 2 2. The value of the gift is the difference between the fair market value of the accommodations and the sums paid by agency officials. As the need arises, these boards contract with hotels by renting a large room or rooms where the examination will be administered. A board staffer negotiates these written contracts. Appropriate contract forms are completed for the rental of the examination rooms and submitted to the business services office of the Department of Consumer Affairs for processing and approval. Concurrently, boards negotiate a rate for sleeping accommodations at the same hotel for board members, staff and proctors involved in the administration of the examination. The hotel either offers a discount account for the sleeping accommodations or provides complimentary accommodations for board members, staff and proctors because of the number of rooms being committed for the examination. This arrangement is oral. The sleeping accommodations are not addressed in the written contract forms completed for the rental of examination rooms. Your agency reimburses board members, staff and proctors at a fixed rate on a per diem basis. Examination candidates also typically arrange for sleeping accommodations at the same hotel. Examination candidates are not provided discounted rooms.
